revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:شيون ان]][[de:Chion-in]][[en:Chion-in]][[es:Chion-in]][[eu:Chion-in]][[fa:معبد چیئون]][[fr:Chion-in]][[gan:知恩院]][[hu:Csionin]][[id:Chion-in]][[ja:知恩院]][[ko:지온인]][[my:ချိအွန်းအင်း]][[or:ଚିଓନ-ଇନ]][[pl:Chion-in]][[ru:Тион-ин]][[tr:Chion-in]][[uk:Монастир Тіон]][[zh:知恩院]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Chion-in (7151259687).jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Jōdo-shū|qid=Q912208|1=Jōdo-shū}}|prefecture={{ill|Higashiyama-ku|qid=Q1203862|1=Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.005208333333|N|135.78340277778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Genchi|qid=Q11564120|1=Genchi}}; {{ill|Hōnen|qid=Q507980|1=Hōnen}}|part_of={{ill|The Twenty-Fifth Reikyo of Dharan Shonin|qid=Q11555252|1=The Twenty-Fifth Reikyo of Dharan Shonin}}}}
{{Infobox religious building | name = Chion-in | native_name = 知恩院 | religious_affiliation = [[Jōdo-shū]] | image = Chion-in (Mieido).jpg | caption = Chion-in's main hall "Mieido" (御影堂) | location = 400 Rinka-cho, {{ill|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|en|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1203862|lt=Higashiyama-ku|1=Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, 605-8686 | deity = [[Amitābha|Amida Buddha]] | country = {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | website = {{URL|https://www.chion-in.or.jp/en/|qq=}} | founded_by = {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|qid=Q507980|lt=Hōnen|1=Hōnen}} }} {{nihongo|'''Chion-in'''|知恩院|Monastery of Gratitude|qq=}} in {{ill|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|en|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1203862|lt=Higashiyama-ku|1=Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} is the headquarters of the [[Jōdo-shū]] (Pure Land Sect) founded by {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|qid=Q507980|lt=Hōnen|1=Hōnen}} (1133–1212), who proclaimed that sentient beings are reborn in [[Amitābha|Amida Buddha]]'s {{ill|Sukhavati|en|Sukhavati|qid=Q1329565|lt=Western Paradise|1=Sukhavati}} (Pure Land) by reciting the ''{{ill|Nianfo|en|Nianfo|qid=Q1315754|lt=Nianfo|1=Nianfo}}'', Amida Buddha's name. The vast compounds of Chion-in include the site where Hōnen settled to disseminate his teachings and the site where he died. == History == [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|Sanmon (National Treasure of Japan)]] The original temple was built in 1234 by Hōnen's disciple, Genchi (1183–1238) in memory of his master and was named Chion-in. While the temple was affiliated more closely in the early years with the {{ill|Seizan|en|Seizan|qid=Q7447036|lt=Seizan|1=Seizan}} branch of Jodo Shu, its 8th head priest, Nyoichi (1262–1321) was deeply influenced by the priest Ryōkū, a disciple of Ryōchū who was the 3rd head of the {{ill|Chinzei|en|Chinzei|qid=Q2963795|lt=Chinzei|1=Chinzei}} branch of Jōdo-shū Buddhism, and disciple of {{ill|Benchō|en|Benchō|qid=Q4886957|lt=Benchō|1=Benchō}}. Later Nyoichi's successor Shunjō (1255–1355) advanced this further by citing a biography where Genchi's disciple Renjaku-bo and Ryōchū agree that there existed no doctrinal differences between them:<ref name="dobbins">{{cite book | last = Dobbins | first = James C. | title = Jodo Shinshu: Shin Buddhism in Medieval Japan | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 2002 | isbn = 0-8248-2620-5 | pages=103–104|qq=}}</ref><ref name="jodo shu">{{cite book |author1=Jonathan Watts |author2=Yoshiharu Tomatsu | title = Traversing the Pure Land Path: A Lifetime of Encounters with Honen Shonin | publisher = Jodo Shu Press | year = 2005 | isbn = 4-88363-342-X | pages=152–153|qq=}}</ref> {{Quote|Then Renjaku-bo said, «There is complete agreement between what Genchi and Bencho say in their basic teaching. So my disciples should from now on look at the Chinzei teaching as their own. There is no need to set up another school.»|qq=}} By 1450, Chion-in had become fully under control of the Chinzei branch, but had little direct control, due to the outbreak of the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}.<ref name="dobbins"/> Numerous buildings in the complex were burnt down in 1633, but were entirely rebuilt by the third [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|qid=Q314481|lt=Iemitsu|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} (1604–1651) with the palatial structures that stand today. == Architecture == [[File:Chionin kamon.jpg|thumb|The Tokugawa family [[:en:Kamon (crest)|Kamon (crest)]] can be seen in the ends of the {{ill|Roof tiles|en|Roof tiles|qid=Q268547|lt=Roof tiles|1=Roof tiles}}, honoring their patronage of the temple.|left]] The colossal main gate, the [[Sanmon]], was built in 1619 and is the largest surviving structure of its kind in Japan. It features an Irimoya-zukuri or a Hip and Gabled roof.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.chion-in.or.jp/en/jodoshu/|title=About Buddhist Temples and Jōdo Shū｜CHION-IN|last=CHION-IN|website=CHION-IN|language=en|access-date=2019-11-20|qq=}}</ref> The purpose of the hip and gable roof is to protect the center point of the temple. Chion-in has a large and a small guest house in the [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] roof style called Ohojo and Kohojo that are designated Important Cultural Heritages. Both guest houses were built in 1641. Chion-in is home to Japan's largest {{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|qid=Q8192099|lt=temple bell|1=Bonshō}}, which was commissioned in 1633 and weighs 74 tons. It used to require a 25-man team to sound it.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Lafcadio Hearn Glimpses of Unfamiliar Japan Vol 1|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PpraAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA61|publisher=Brighthouse|pages=61 (footnote)|id=GGKEY:94BGWZS0H8R|qq=}}</ref> But now the temple website says 17 are needed. There are a few interesting features to note about Chion-in: first, all roof beams are carved with the family crest of the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa]] family: three hollyhock leaves. Another feature is the umbrella found stashed in the rafters outside the main temple. One of the architects who helped rebuild the temple placed the umbrella in the rafters to help bring rain (and thereby ward off fire).[[File:Chionin garden.jpg|thumb|The pond behind Chion-in]] An interesting feature inside the temple is the very squeaky boards, an example of a {{ill|Nightingale floor|en|Nightingale floor|qid=Q1471563|lt=Nightingale floor|1=Nightingale floor}}. The wooden boards were built with metal ends that would rub against the metal joints they were attached to, created a piercing noise as people step on them. This was intentionally done so that when the Tokugawa family stayed at the temple, they could detect unwanted intruders at night. The interior of Chion-in is decorated with black, gold and cinnabar colors throughout. A baldacchino of fine metal work, and red, silk ropes and tassels adorn the area. Lotus plants in pots encased in beaten gold.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Cram|first=Ralph Adams|date=1905|title=Impressions of Japanese Architecture and the Allied Arts|journal=The Collector and Art Critic|volume=4|issue=2|pages=55|doi=10.2307/25435595|issn=1948-0202|jstor=25435595|hdl=2027/gri.ark:/13960/t45r1g748|url=http://archive.org/details/impressionsofjap00cramiala |hdl-access=free|qq=}}</ref> The introduction of wood being a fine art and working with contemporary art is drastically changed the way that religious buildings were built and decorated. == 7 Wonders == === Uguisubari-no-rōka === This is the Nightingale Hallway (Buddha's Vow). This 550m long hallway connects Mieid{{ill|Benchō|en|Benchō|qid=Q4886957|lt=ō|1=Benchō}} and Shūed{{ill|Benchō|en|Benchō|qid=Q4886957|lt=ō|1=Benchō}}. The Nightingale hallway was equipped with flooring that make the sound of the Nightingale when someone walks on them. It acts as a type of security system as the lighter someone tries to walk the more sound it makes.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://www.chion-in.or.jp/en/highlight/wonders.php|title=The Seven Wonders of Chion-in - History & highlights｜CHION-IN|last=CHION-IN|website=CHION-IN|language=en|access-date=2019-11-21|qq=}}</ref> They say that the sound of the nightingale bird is meant to be a reminder to listen to Buddha's teachings.<ref name=":0" /> === Shiraki-no-hitsugi === This is the Plainwood Coffin (A symbol of Nonattachment to One's Life and Body). In the Sanmon, the bodies of Gomi Kin'uemon and his wife lie in plain coffins. These people built the gate and carved wooden statues of themselves. After the gate was completed, both Gomi Kin'uemon and his wife committed suicide. People still to this day who visit Chion-in cry for them.<ref name=":0" /> === Wasuregasa === This is the Forgotten Umbrella (A Symbol of Gratitude). This umbrella holds many different meanings, it is in Mieidō between the eaves on the southeast section. It is said to be a symbol of gratitude delivered by a white fox, promising to protect Chion-in. Another said it was simply forgotten by master carpenter, Hidari Jingorō. Lasty, it is said to protect Chion-in from fires because it has a connected relationship to water.<ref name=":0" /> === Nukesuzume === This is the Sparrows that Flew Away (A Symbol of Polishing One's Mind). The fusuma-e, or a painting on sliding doors, done by Kanō Nobumasa a painting featuring white Chrysanthemum flowers. According to legend sparrows were painted on but the birds look so real that they came to life and flew away.<ref name=":0" /> === Sanpō Shōmen Mamuki-no-Neko === This is the Cat That Sees in Three Directions (A Symbol of a Parent’s Heart). Located in Ōhōjō, the large guest house. This painting of a cat represents compassion of the Buddha and the heart of a parent. This cat seems to be looking at you at anywhere you may be look at it.<ref name=":0" /> === Ōshakushi === This is the Large Rice Pa11le (A Symbol of the Buddha’s Salvation). This was a larger than average pa11le, meant to serve a lot of people at once. This rice pa11le, according to legend, was meant to save all of mankind. "Scoop" and "Save/rescue" are both pronounced Sukuu. This play on words was meant to symbolize Amida's compassion.<ref name=":0" /> === Uryūseki === This is the Cucumber Rock (A Symbol of Encouragement). This large rock said to predate Chion-in is in front of the Kuromon. Gozu Tennō, a deity, said that in one night gourds started to grow from this rock.<ref name=":0" /> ==location== Directly to the north of Chion-in (and abutting) is the much smaller temple of {{ill|Shōren-in|en|Shōren-in|qid=Q377386|lt=Shōren-in|1=Shōren-in}}. Directly to the south (and abutting) is {{ill|Maruyama Park|en|Maruyama Park|qid=Q1108695|lt=Maruyama Park|1=Maruyama Park}}, which itself connects to [[Yasaka Shrine]] and thence [[Gion]]. ==Gallery== <gallery> File:Wooden gate with open double door leading to the garden of Isshinin Buddhist temple in the compounds of Chion-in Kyoto Japan.jpg|View on the garden of ''Isshinin'' {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples in Japan|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} File:Chionin31n3200.jpg|Ōhōjō File:Chionin02n4272.jpg|[[Dō (architecture)|Amida-dō]] File:Chionin11n4272.jpg|Seishi-dō File:Chion-in - various - 20150621 - 06.jpg|[[Tahōtō]] (pagoda) File:Chion-in - various - 20150621 - 05.jpg File:Chionin sanmon gate.jpg|The front gate or {{nihongo|''sanmon''|山門||lit. "Mountain Gate"|qq=}} of Chion-in File:Rapid descent.jpg|{{ill|Raigō of Amida and Twenty-five Attendants|en|Raigō of Amida and Twenty-five Attendants|qid=Q107046356|lt=Raigō of Amida and Twenty-five Attendants|1=Raigō of Amida and Twenty-five Attendants}} - a painting by the temple that is designated a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} </gallery> ==See also== * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|qid=Q3245523|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|qid=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Pure Land Buddhism|en|Pure Land Buddhism|qid=Q262244|lt=Pure Land Buddhism|1=Pure Land Buddhism}} ==Notes== {{Reflist|qq=}} ==References== * {{ill|James C. Dobbins|en|James C. Dobbins|qid=Q6130690|lt=Dobbins, James C.|1=James C. Dobbins}} (1989). ''Jodo Shinshu: Shin Buddhism in Medieval Japan.'' Bloomington, Illinois: Indiana University Press. {{ISBN|9780253331861|qq=}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/470742039 OCLC 470742039] == External links == {{Commons Category|Chion-in|qq=}} * [http://www.chion-in.or.jp/e/ Homepage of Chion-in] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100203182921/http://www.chion-in.or.jp/e/ |date=2010-02-03 |qq=}} in English * [http://japanwallpaper.flowersentence.com/?eid=1323097 Information and Photograph] in English *[https://www.flickr.com/photos/141143692@N07/albums/72157678353874890 Album of 2016 Photographs] on {{ill|Flickr|en|Flickr|qid=Q103204|lt=Flickr|1=Flickr}} {{Coord|35|00|23|N|135|47|02|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title|qq=}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan|qq=}}
[[en:Chion-in]]
